cards.text: Class definitions should go in {{c1::header files}} (.h) and member function definitions should go in {{c2::source files}} (.cpp).
To define a member function outside the class, use the {{c1::scope resolution operator}}: {{c2::ClassName::}}functionName()
Member functions defined inside the class definition are implicitly {{c1::inline}}, which may allow the compiler to expand them in place.
Trivial member functions (like simple {{c1::getters}} and {{c2::setters}}) can be defined {{c3::inside the class definition}}, while non-trivial functions should be defined {{c4::in the .cpp file}}.
Constructors with default parameters can have the default values specified in the {{c1::class definition}} but not in the {{c2::function definition}}.

cards.front: What are the two ways to define a member function?
cards.back:
• Inside the class definition (implicitly inline)
  • Outside the class using the scope resolution operator (ClassName::)

cards.front: Why should non-trivial member functions be defined in .cpp files instead of header files?
cards.back: To minimize recompilation when the implementation changes. Changes to .cpp files only require recompiling that file, while changes to .h files require recompiling all files that include them.

cards.question: How do you define this member function outside the class?
cards.answer: void Date::print() const { std::cout << m_year << '/' << m_month << '/' << m_day; }
cards.explanation: Use the scope resolution operator Date:: before the function name, and include the const qualifier.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Cannot use the scope resolution operator (Date::) when declaring a member function inside the class definition.
cards.explanation: The scope resolution operator is only used when defining member functions outside the class, not when declaring them inside.

cards.question: Which of these member functions is implicitly inline?
cards.answer: Version 1 is implicitly inline (defined inside the class).
cards.explanation: Member functions defined inside the class definition are implicitly inline. Version 2 is not inline unless explicitly marked.

cards.concept: Best practice for organizing class code
cards.explanation:
Separate class declarations from definitions:
  • Header file (.h): Class definition with member declarations
  • Source file (.cpp): Member function definitions
  • Exception: Trivial functions (simple getters/setters) can stay in the header
  • Benefit: Faster recompilation when implementation changes

cards.back: Scope resolution operator, used to access members of a namespace or define class members outside the class definition
